query: evidence on suicidal behavior differences between unipolar depression and bipolar disorder

retrieved_docs.title: Suicidal Ideation -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Mood disorders: This includes major depressive disorder, bipolar disorder (both manic and depressive phases), and dysthymia. Mood disorders are strongly associated with suicidal ideations.

retrieved_docs.title: Suicide Risk -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholism Anxiety disorders Bipolar disorder Delirium Depression Personality disorders Posttraumatic stress disorders Schizophrenia

retrieved_docs.title: Suicide: Assessment and Management -- Treatment / Management -- Evidence-Based Somatic Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: Lithium significantly reduces the risk of suicide compared to placebo or no intervention and is especially effective for individuals with bipolar disorder. Antiepileptic mood stabilizers were associated with a higher risk of suicide when compared to lithium. [53] [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Bipolar Disorder -- Treatment / Management -- Acute Bipolar Depression
retrieved_docs.content: Suicidal and self-harm risk has priority in managing patients with bipolar disorder who present with an acute depressive episode because most suicide deaths in patients with BD occur during this phase. Patients may or may not require hospitalization.

retrieved_docs.title: Suicide: Assessment and Management -- History and Physical -- Estimation of Suicide Risk
retrieved_docs.content: Any mental disorder, including bipolar disorder, depression, anxiety, schizophrenia, substance use disorders, and eating disorders, especially with increased impairment

retrieved_docs.title: Bipolar Disorder -- Treatment / Management -- Acute Bipolar Depression
retrieved_docs.content: Consider cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) as an add-on to pharmacotherapy. However, never consider CBT as monotherapy because there is minimal evidence to support psychological treatments without pharmacotherapy in treating acute bipolar depression. Also, consider adding ECT for refractory bipolar depression or as a first-line treatment in the presence of psychotic features and a high risk of suicide.

retrieved_docs.title: Bipolar Disorder -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Bipolar disorder is one of the top 10 leading causes of disability worldwide. [31] A recent meta-analysis showed that patients with BD “experienced reduced life expectancy relative to the general population, with approximately 13 years of potential life lost.” Additionally, patients with bipolar disorder showed a greater reduction in lifespan relative to the general population than patients with common mental health disorders, including anxiety and depressive disorders, and life expectancy was significantly lower in men with BD than in women with BD. [32] A different meta-analysis showed that all-cause mortality in patients with BD is double that expected in the general population. Natural deaths occurred over 1.5 times greater in BD, comprised of an “almost double risk of deaths from circulatory illnesses (heart attacks, strokes, etc) and 3 times the risk of deaths from respiratory illness (COPD, asthma, etc).” Unnatural deaths occurred approximately 7 times more often than in the general population, with an increased suicide risk of approximately 14 times and an increased risk of other violent deaths of almost 4 times. Deaths by all causes studied were similarly increased in men and women. [33] A more recent systematic review of the association between completed suicide and bipolar disorder showed an approximately 20- to 30-fold greater suicide rate in bipolar disorder than in the general population. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Myers-Briggs Type Indicator -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Although the MBTI was not originally designed for clinical use, it has been applied to certain patient populations. In psychology and psychiatry, the MBTI may provide insights into specific groups, such as those experiencing suicidality and unipolar depression. Research has shown that these populations tend to exhibit greater tendencies toward introversion in energy and perceiving in orientation than the general population. Researchers suggest that, with further confirmatory studies, these correlations could help identify vulnerability in patients with affective disorders. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Washington State Suicide Prevention and Awareness -- Function -- Medications and Suicides
retrieved_docs.content: Individuals with bipolar disorder are at very high risk for suicidal behavior, especially females who had the onset of symptoms early in life. Several studies have shown that individuals with bipolar disorders have a high rate of attempted suicides. Besides the manic phase and impulsive behavior, these individuals can also develop severe depression that must also be considered a risk for suicide. Other comorbid factors that increase the risk of suicide in bipolar individuals include the presence of anxiety disorder and a family history of suicide. Fortunately, many international studies have shown that lithium is known to possess anti-suicidal effects in bipolar disorder. The only problem is that lithium has a very low therapeutic index, and its use has to be closely monitored. Compliance with lithium therapy is also a major issue among bipolar patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Bipolar Disorder -- Evaluation -- DSM-5 Diagnostic Criteria for Bipolar II Disorder
retrieved_docs.content: A hypomanic episode is defined as a distinct period of persistently elevated or irritable mood with increased activity or energy lasting for at least 4 consecutive days. The presence of 3 or more of the following is required to qualify as a hypomanic episode. If the mood is irritable, at least 4 of the following must be present: Inflated self-esteem or grandiosity Decreased need for sleep A compulsion to keep talking or being more talkative than usual Flight of ideas or racing thoughts High distractibility Increased goal-directed activity (socially, at work or school, or sexually) or psychomotor agitation (non-goal-directed activity) Excessive involvement in activities that have a high potential for painful consequences, such as engaging in unrestrained buying sprees, sexual indiscretions, or foolish business investments

retrieved_docs.title: Suicidal Ideation -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Underlying causes and severity: The prognosis is generally more favorable if the suicidal ideation is linked to situational factors that can be resolved, such as acute stress or a specific problem that has a solution. In cases where suicidal ideation is a symptom of a chronic mental health disorder, such as MDD or bipolar disorder, the prognosis depends on the severity of the disorder and the patient's response to treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Suicide Screening and Prevention -- Function -- Medications and Suicides
retrieved_docs.content: Individuals with bipolar disorder are at very high risk for suicidal behavior, especially females who had the onset of symptoms early in life. Several studies have shown that individuals with bipolar disorders have a high rate of attempted suicides. Besides the manic phase and impulsive behavior, these individuals can also develop severe depression, that must also a risk for suicide. Other comorbid factors that increase the risk of suicide in bipolar individuals include the presence of anxiety disorder and a family history of suicide. Fortunately, many international studies have shown that lithium is known to possess anti-suicidal effects in bipolar disorder. The only problem is that lithium has a very low therapeutic index, and its use has to be closely monitored. Compliance with lithium therapy is also a major issue among bipolar patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Bipolar Disorder -- Evaluation -- DSM-5 Diagnostic Criteria for Bipolar I Disorder
retrieved_docs.content: A manic episode is defined as a distinct period of persistently elevated or irritable mood with increased activity or energy lasting for at least 7 consecutive days or requiring hospitalization. The presence of 3 or more of the following is required to qualify as a manic episode. If the mood is irritable, at least 4 of the following must be present: Inflated self-esteem or grandiosity Decreased need for sleep A compulsion to keep talking or being more talkative than usual Flight of ideas or racing thoughts High distractibility Increased goal-directed activity (socially, at work or school, or sexually) or psychomotor agitation (non-goal-directed activity) Excessive involvement in activities that have a high potential for painful consequences, such as engaging in unrestrained buying sprees, sexual indiscretions, or foolish business investments The episode is not attributable to the physiological effects of a substance or general medical condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Suicide Screening and Prevention -- Function -- Which type of mental health disorder is associated with an increased risk of suicide?
retrieved_docs.content: Accumulated data reveal that many types of mental health disorders have been associated with an increased risk of suicide, and they include the following: Major depression Schizophrenia Substance abuse Alcoholism Post-traumatic stress disorder Bipolar disorder Personality disorders Emotional stress

retrieved_docs.title: Major Depressive Disorder -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Untreated depressive episodes in major depressive disorder can last from 6 to 12 months. About two-thirds of the individuals with MDD contemplate suicide, and about 10 to 15 percent commit suicide. MDD is a chronic, recurrent illness; the recurrence rate is about 50% after the first episode, 70% after the second episode, and 90% after the third episode. About 5 to 10 percent of the patients with MDD eventually develop bipolar disorder. [11] The prognosis of MDD is good in patients with mild episodes, the absence of psychotic symptoms, better treatment compliance, a strong support system, and good premorbid functioning. The prognosis is poor in the presence of a comorbid psychiatric disorder, personality disorder, multiple hospitalizations, and advanced age of onset.

retrieved_docs.title: Bipolar Disorder -- Evaluation -- The presence of 5 or more of the following symptoms daily or nearly every day for a consecutive 2-week period that is a change from baseline or previous functioning:
retrieved_docs.content: Subjective report of depressed mood most of the day (or depressed mood observed by others) Anhedonia most of the day Significant weight loss when not dieting or weight gain or decrease or increase in appetite Insomnia or hypersomnia Psychomotor agitation or retardation Fatigue or loss of energy Feelings of worthlessness or excessive or inappropriate guilt Decreased concentration or indecisiveness Recurrent thoughts of death, recurrent suicidal ideation without a specific plan

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 534
retrieved_docs.content: Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere are positioned within both the mood disorders and anxiety disorders categories, reflecting that this diagnostic group exhibits features common to each of these major psychiatric domains. In a similar vein, the Transient organic psychotic condition, depressive type is closely linked to mood disorders and may be regarded as essentially synonymous with them, underscoring its classification as a mood‑related disorder. Together, these relationships highlight the overlapping nature of certain transient mental health conditions across traditional mood and anxiety disorder boundaries, which is important for accurate diagnosis and appropriate treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 435
retrieved_docs.content: The classification “الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ)” is presented as a broad grouping that encompasses a variety of psychotic and affective conditions. Within this group are several specific entities: “ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة” (unspecified childhood psychosis, current or active), “هوس شَبَقِيّ” (schizotypal mania), and “عاطفة متقلصة” (blunted affect). In addition to primary psychotic disorders, the SMQ grouping also includes mood‑related diagnoses such as “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة” (major depressive disorder, recurrent episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة” (major depressive disorder, single episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة” (major depressive disorder, single episode in full remission), “اكتئاب أَوبِيّ” (atypical depression), and “التقلقل الوجداني” (affective lability). This reflects an integrated view in which depressive disorders and affective symptoms are considered part of the broader psychosis spectrum, especially when they may present with psychotic features. The entity “ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة” is also linked to the more general category “disorders usually diagnosed in infancy,” indicating that this form of childhood psychosis is typically identified early in life. Furthermore, “هوس شَبَقِيّ” is related to “ذُهان عضوي” (organic psychosis) through a broader‑relationship link, suggesting that schizotypal mania can be viewed as a subtype or manifestation of an organic psychotic process. Overall, the SMQ framework integrates early‑onset psychotic conditions, organic psychoses, specific manic presentations, and a range of depressive and affective disorders, highlighting the clinical overlap among psychotic and mood disturbances and underscoring the importance of considering these interrelationships in diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2279
retrieved_docs.content: Depression (excluding suicide and self‑harm) (SMQ) is presented as a broad clinical category that includes several distinct subtypes and related conditions. Within this category, reactive depression (اكتئاب تفاعلي), endogenous depression (اكتئاب داخلي المنشأ), melancholic depression (اكتئاب سوداوي) and habituation (الاعتياد) are identified as specific forms of depressive illness. The same overarching depression concept also encompasses postpartum depression (excluding psychosis) (الاكتئاب التالي للولادة (باستثناء الذُهان)), emotional poverty (الفقر العاطفي), heightened vigilance (اليقظة), alcohol poisoning (تسمم كحولي), marijuana use (تعاطي المارجوانا), mood swings (تقلب الحالة المزاجية), memory impairment (خلل في الذاكرة), psychological distress (ضائقة نفسية) and loss of ambition (فقدان الطموح). By grouping these diverse depressive presentations, substance‑related effects, and psychosocial states under a single classification, clinicians can better recognize the varied manifestations of depression, understand their interrelationships, and tailor assessment and treatment strategies to address the full spectrum of depressive disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 196
retrieved_docs.content: Depression (excluding suicide and self‑harm) (SMQ) is described as a heterogeneous clinical entity that incorporates a wide spectrum of depressive subtypes, affective and cognitive symptoms, sleep‑wake disturbances, substance‑related contexts, and therapeutic interventions. The major depressive disorder subtypes—“اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة” (recurrent episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة” (single episode), and “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة” (single episode in full remission)—are grouped with additional forms such as “اكتئاب أَوبِيّ,” “اكتئاب تفاعلي” (reactive depression), “اكتئاب داخلي المنشأ” (endogenous depression), and “اكتئاب سوداوي” (melancholic depression). Emotional manifestations include emotional lability (“التقلقل الوجداني”), guilt (“الشعور بالذنب”), emotional poverty (“الفقر العاطفي”), indifference (“اللامبالاة”), heightened vigilance (“اليقظة”), and mood swings (“تقلب الحالة المزاجية”). Cognitive‑behavioral features encompass attention deficit,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 662
retrieved_docs.content: Depression (excluding suicide and self‑harm) (SMQ) is presented as an overarching clinical category that encompasses a wide range of specific depressive subtypes, related conditions, and associated phenomena. Within this category, reactive depression (اكتئاب تفاعلي), endogenous depression (اكتئاب داخلي المنشأ), melancholic depression (اكتئاب سوداوي) and habituation (الاعتياد) are identified as distinct members, reflecting different etiological and symptomatic profiles. Post‑partum depression (الاكتئاب التالي للولادة (باستثناء الذُهان)) is also classified under the same SMQ, highlighting its relevance as a specific temporal form of depressive illness. Therapeutic interventions such as electroconvulsive therapy (العلاج بالتخليج الكهربي) are linked to the SMQ, indicating their use in managing severe depressive states. A variety of psychosocial and neurobehavioral factors are likewise incorporated, including emotional poverty (الفقر العاطفي), heightened vigilance (اليقظة), alcohol poisoning (تسمم كحولي), marijuana use (تعاطي المارجوانا), mood swings (تقلب الحالة المزاجية), memory impairment (خلل في الذاكرة), psychological distress (ضائقة نفسية) and loss of ambition (فقدان الطموح). Together, these entities illustrate the complex, multifactorial nature of depression as captured by the SMQ framework, emphasizing the importance of recognizing diverse presentations and contributing factors in clinical assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 962
retrieved_docs.content: The category “disorders usually diagnosed in infancy” has a broader relationship with ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة (unspecified childhood psychosis, current or active). This specific psychotic condition is a member of the larger classification الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) (psychosis and psychotic disorders). Within the الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) group, several mood‑related diagnoses are also listed as members: اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة (major depressive disorder, recurrent episode), اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة (major depressive disorder, single episode), اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة (major depressive disorder, single episode in full remission), اكتئاب أَوبِيّ (atypical depression), and the symptom التقلقل الوجداني (mood lability). Moreover, the entire الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) category is itself a member of عاطفة متقلصة (blunted affect). This network of relationships illustrates the overlap between early‑onset psychotic conditions, broader psychotic disorder classifications, depressive mood disorders, and affective symptoms such as blunted affect, emphasizing the need for integrated assessment and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 548
retrieved_docs.content: Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere is classified as a child category of mood disorders, indicating that it falls within the broader spectrum of mood‑related conditions. Likewise, the transient organic psychotic condition, depressive type is described as being closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, mood disorders, suggesting that it shares core features with other mood disorder diagnoses. Together, these relationships highlight that both the specified transient mental disorders and the depressive‑type transient organic psychotic condition are encompassed within the mood disorders domain, underscoring their relevance for diagnostic classification and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 964
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition is defined using the same diagnostic criteria as anxiety disorder due to known physiological condition, and these criteria are incorporated within the broader diagnostic framework for anxiety disorders. The entity Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere is classified under both mood disorders and anxiety disorders, reflecting its dual nature as a transient condition that can present with features of each category. Additionally, Transient organic psychotic condition, depressive type is closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, mood disorders, indicating that its depressive psychotic presentation is viewed as a variant within the mood‑disorder spectrum. Together, these relationships illustrate the overlapping boundaries among anxiety‑related, mood‑related, and transient psychotic conditions, emphasizing the importance of shared diagnostic criteria and cross‑categorical classification in clinical assessment and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.content: and miscellaneous mental health disorders, reflecting their multifaceted nature that spans both affective and anxiety symptom domains while also belonging to the miscellaneous category. The transient organic psychotic condition, depressive type, is described as related to and possibly synonymous with mood disorders, indicating that depressive‑type transient psychoses are viewed as part of the mood disorder spectrum. Collectively, these relationships portray a hierarchical and intersecting structure in which drug‑induced and medically‑based anxiety and mood disorders are integrated within broader categories of organic and miscellaneous mental health disorders, sharing diagnostic criteria and conceptual overlap that guide clinical classification and patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Drug‑induced mood disorder is classified as a form of اضطراب الحالة المزاجية العضوي, meaning an organic mood disorder; it denotes mood disturbances that arise directly from the pharmacological effects of substances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2287
retrieved_docs.content: Major depressive disorder with a recurrent episode, major depressive disorder with a single episode, major depressive disorder with a single episode in full remission, atypical depression (اكتئاب أوبِيّ), and mood lability (التقلقل الوجداني) are all classified as members of the schizophrenia and psychotic disorders SMQ (الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ)). The SMQ itself is categorized under the broader construct of blunted affect (عاطفة متقلصة). This hierarchical organization indicates that these depressive conditions are considered within the spectrum of schizophrenia‑related and psychotic disorders, and that the entire group is associated with a reduction in emotional expressivity. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians appreciate the overlap between mood disturbances and psychotic features, and underscores the importance of assessing affective flattening when evaluating patients with these diagnoses.
